topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The photograph, faded at the edges like old parchment, felt heavy in my palm. Liam, grinning wildly in a sun-drenched field, his arm slung casually around my shoulder. I traced the outline of his smile, a slow, shallow breath escaping my lips. I found him at the coffee shop, the one we used to haunt after school, and he was engrossed in a book, oblivious. When I finally approached, my voice came out thin, a fragile sound. "Hey, Liam. Remember that summer by the lake?" He looked up, his eyes clear and kind, but there was a blankness there, a smooth surface where memory should have been turbulent. "I... I don't think so. Who are you?" The air seemed to thin around me. My hands clenched into fists inside my coat pockets, the knuckles turning white. I managed a weak smile, a performance I couldn't sustain, and turned to walk away, the sound of the coffee grinder suddenly deafening.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the way his gaze had shifted, the sudden, startling lack of recognition. It felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I sat on the edge of the park bench, the cool metal biting into my legs. Every shadow seemed too deep, every rustle of leaves sounded like judgment. I pulled out my phone, scrolling through old messages, searching for any flicker of shared history, any accidental thread connecting us. Nothing. Just the silence of unanswered questions echoing in the hollow space where our bond used to reside. A tremor ran through my hands, making the phone slip slightly in my grip. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the churning sensation in my stomach to settle, but it only tightened, a knot of sharp, unwelcome pressure.

[story 3]

"It was just a joke, right?" I asked, my voice pitched unnaturally high, trying to inject lightness into the suffocating silence. Liam shifted uncomfortably, running a hand through his already messy hair. His expression was one of genuine confusion, the kind you give a stranger who has misremembered a detail. "I honestly can't place it. You sound familiar, though. Like... a distant echo." The word 'echo' landed like shattered glass in my chest. I stood up abruptly, needing distance, needing to escape the suffocating closeness of his confusion. My steps were uneven, a frantic rhythm against the pavement. I walked until the streetlights blurred, the cold night air stinging my cheeks. I stopped under a flickering lamp, leaning against the brick wall, and let out a shaky exhale, the effort of holding my composure finally dissolving into a silent, desperate shaking of my whole frame.
